summary: Drew DeVault announces several projects that were previously blocked pending the public release of the Hare programming language. He introduces Helios (a microkernel), Himitsu (a secret storage system), btqd (a bittorrent daemon), and scheduled (a cron replacement), all written in Hare. The post also covers SourceHut progress on data portability and account import/export, plus significant Hare ecosystem developments including an OpenBSD port, new crypto modules, and JSON support. The overall theme is one of creative momentum unleashed by Hare's public availability.
key_points: Hare's public release unblocked multiple projects that required a suitable systems language
Helios is an L4-inspired microkernel targeting x86_64 with plans for aarch64 and riscv64, already running code in userspace
Himitsu reimagines secret storage beyond passwords, with extensible key/value pairs and agent protocols for SSH keys and other credentials
SourceHut is building comprehensive data import/export so users can take all their data to any compatible platform
Over 300 commits landed in Hare in a single month, including an OpenBSD port, crypto primitives, and JSON support
money_quotes: The Hare programming language is a secret no more!
One thing Hare has done is allow me to unshelve several projects which were blocked pending the availability of a suitable language to write them in.
I have never been thrilled with cron's design — it's very un-Unix like.

This will allow you to easily take all of your data out of sourcehut and import it into another instance, or any compatible software — your git repos are just git repos and your mailing lists are just mbox files.

As of this morning, I have some code running in userspace — one variant attempts to cli, causing a general protection fault (as expected), and another just runs a busy loop, which works without any faults.
key_insight: The public release of Hare catalyzed an entire ecosystem of ambitious systems projects — from microkernels to secret managers — that had been waiting for the right language to exist.
